title: Carbohydrate Calculator for Exercise | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Many Carbohydrates Do You Need During Exercise?
description: Calculate carbohydrate needs by duration and intensity. Get personalized recommendations based on ACSM guidelines for runners, cyclists, and endurance athletes. Free calculator.
intro: Sports training is built on physiological principles—adaptation, supercompensation, and specificity. Training zones, weekly load, and recovery are the pillars of performance. This calculator helps you determine how many grams of carbohydrates per hour you need during exercise based on duration and intensity. Our recommendations follow ACSM guidelines and are updated regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: **<60 min**: no carbs needed. **60-150 min**: 30-60 g/hour. **>150 min**: 60-90 g/hour (dual-transport: glucose + fructose). One gel ≈ 25g carbs.
useCases: Running a half marathon and need to plan your gels.
Going on long bike rides and want to calculate your nutrition strategy.
Training for 90+ minutes and feeling depleted—time to refuel.
Building a nutrition plan for a trail race.
Wondering if you need to eat during your CrossFit session.

faq.q: Do I need carbs if I exercise for less than an hour?
faq.a: Generally no. Your glycogen stores are sufficient for 60-90 minutes of moderate exercise. For shorter workouts, a solid pre-workout meal is enough.

faq.q: What's the best type of carb to eat during exercise?
faq.a: Simple, fast-absorbing carbs: gels, sports drinks, bananas, or honey. For efforts exceeding 2.5 hours, use a glucose + fructose blend (2:1 ratio) to absorb up to 90g per hour.

faq.q: How often should I consume carbs during a long workout?
faq.a: Every 20-30 minutes, starting around 45-60 minutes into your workout. Frequent, small amounts prevent GI distress better than one large dose.

faq.q: How many grams of carbs are in a sports gel?
faq.a: Most contain 20-30g of carbs. Common brands (GU, Maurten, SiS) have about 25g. Always check the label for your specific product.

faq.q: Can I use regular Coca-Cola as a carb source during exercise?
faq.a: Yes, it works in a pinch—especially in ultramarathons. About 100 ml has 10.6g carbs plus caffeine. It's less convenient than gels but it works.

faq.q: Does fructose cause stomach problems during exercise?
faq.a: Excessive fructose can. Fructose absorbs differently than glucose. Mixed 2:1 with glucose, it allows more total carbs per hour without digestive issues.

faq.q: Do I need to practice eating while exercising?
faq.a: Yes, absolutely. Gut tolerance to eating during exercise is trainable. Start with small amounts during training, then gradually increase before race day.

faq.q: Why is the glucose-to-fructose ratio important for long efforts?
faq.a: Glucose and fructose use different absorption pathways. The 2:1 ratio maximizes total carb absorption (up to 90g per hour) without overwhelming your stomach.

faq.q: What's the difference between carb-loading before a race and eating carbs during?
faq.a: Pre-race carb-loading maximizes your glycogen stores before the start. Carbs during exercise replace energy you're burning. Both are necessary for endurance events.

faq.q: How do I know if I'm consuming the right amount of carbs during exercise?
faq.a: If you're hitting the wall or running out of energy before your goal, you're likely underfueling. If you have GI distress, you may be overfueling. Adjust based on training experience.

sources.name: ACSM - American College of Sports Medicine
ISSN - International Society of Sports Nutrition
NSCA - National Strength and Conditioning Association

explanation:
## How It Works

This calculator determines how many grams of carbohydrates per hour you need during exercise based on duration and intensity, following ACSM (American College of Sports Medicine) guidelines.

Enter your exercise duration, choose your intensity level, and provide your body weight. The calculator shows your carbohydrate needs per hour, total for the session, and practical equivalents like gels or bananas.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (medical, nutritional, or performance-related), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.
